markdownRemark.html:
Big-O Notation

Notes based on Section 0.3 of Algorithms by Dasgupta, Papadimitriou, and Vazirani.

Basic unit of run time

To faciliate discussion of algorithms, we need a way to describe the run time of an algorithm as a function of the size of its input. Consider the following snippet of Python-like pseudocode.

1   nums = [ 1, 2, ..., n ]
2   for i in nums:
3       for j in nums:
4           k = i + j

In the analysis of this code, we cannot effectively describe the run time of the addition and assignment operations from k = i + j in a manner that generalizes across most scenarios. The run time of these operation are dependent on factors outside of the control of the code, including the underlying processor and operating system. To avoid this issue, we employ the abstract unit of a "computer step" for basic operations.

Returning to the analysis, let's treat the array nums with nnn elements as the input to our algorithm. We see that the first for loop repeats nnn times, and the second for loop repeats nnn times. Then, we simplify line 4 to two computer steps: one for addition, and one for assignment. Hence, we find that our algorithm takes n⋅n⋅2=2n2n \cdot n \cdot 2 = 2n^2n⋅n⋅2=2n2 steps overall given an input of size nnn.

What is Big-O?

Let's say that an algorithm takes 4n3+5n2+64n^3+5n^2+64n3+5n2+6 steps. For comparisons of run time between algorithms, it is useful to omit terms that are relatively insignificant to the overall run time. As nnn increases, 5n25n^25n2 and 666 don't grow as fast as 4n34n^34n3, allowing us to ignore the smaller terms and just focus on 4n34n^34n3. Furthermore, we can drop the constant 444 from 4n34n^34n3, making the big-OOO run time O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3). The reason for this simplification becomes more apparent with the precise definition of big-OOO ("big oh") notation:

Given functions f(n)f(n)f(n) and g(n)g(n)g(n) which take positive integer inputs and produce positive real outputs, f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n)) if there exists a constant kkk such that f(n)≤k⋅g(n)f(n) \leq k\cdot g(n)f(n)≤k⋅g(n).

In speech, one would say that "ggg is big oh of fff" to convey that ggg grows at least as fast as fff as the size of the input increases. Now we see why we could drop the constant from 4n34n^34n3 to yield O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3), since the presence of a specific constant coefficient has no effect on the big-OOO relationship of two algorithms. There just has to exist some constant kkk satisfying f(n)≤k⋅g(n)f(n)\leq k \cdot g(n)f(n)≤k⋅g(n) for the relation to exist.

Notation in practice

How does this notation help capture the difference in complexity between two algorithms? Consider the following example:

Let f(n)=n2f(n) = n^2f(n)=n2 and let g(n)=n+100g(n)=n + 100g(n)=n+100. For 0≤n≤100\leq n \leq 100≤n≤10, an algorithm with complexity fff will take fewer steps than ggg does. However, for all n>10n > 10n>10, having complexity fff will necessitate more computer steps than ggg. We can see that g=O(f)g=O(f)g=O(f), since we can establish the following inequality.

g(n)f(n)=n+100n2≤101\frac{g(n)}{f(n)}=\frac{n+100}{n^2}\leq 101f(n)g(n)​=n2n+100​≤101

Attempting to fit f=O(g)f=O(g)f=O(g) into our definition for big-OOO will not work. As nnn increases,

f(n)g(n)=n2n+100=∞\frac{f(n)}{g(n)}=\frac{n^2}{n+100}=\inftyg(n)f(n)​=n+100n2​=∞

indicating that the ratio cannot be upper-bounded with a constant term.

Note that f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n)) does not strictly mean that ggg grows faster than fff, as both f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n)) and g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n)) can be true. Let f(n)=n+10f(n)=n+10f(n)=n+10 and let g(n)=2n+5g(n)=2n+5g(n)=2n+5. We can perform the test from above to show that f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n)) and g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n)):

f(n)g(n)=n+102n+5≤2\frac{f(n)}{g(n)}=\frac{n+10}{2n+5}\leq 2g(n)f(n)​=2n+5n+10​≤2
g(n)f(n)=2n+5n+10<2\frac{g(n)}{f(n)}=\frac{2n+5}{n+10}< 2f(n)g(n)​=n+102n+5​<2

We use big-Θ\ThetaΘ ("big theta") notation to capture this case where f(n)f(n)f(n) and g(n)g(n)g(n) are big-OOO of each other.

Given functions f(n)f(n)f(n) and g(n)g(n)g(n), f(n)=Θ(g(n))f(n)=\Theta(g(n))f(n)=Θ(g(n)) if f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n))f(n)=O(g(n)) and g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n)).

Lastly, big-Ω\OmegaΩ ("big omega") is also used to capture the big-OOO relation in the opposite direction.

Given functions f(n)f(n)f(n) and g(n)g(n)g(n), f(n)=Ω(g(n))f(n)=\Omega(g(n))f(n)=Ω(g(n)) if g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n))g(n)=O(f(n)).

This notation maps roughly to the comparison symbols for greater-than-or-equal-to (≥\geq≥), less-than-or-equal-to (≤\leq≤), and equality (===).

Symbol Big-
≤\leq≤ Big-OOO
≥\geq≥ Big-Ω\OmegaΩ
=== Big-Θ\ThetaΘ

You may want to commit this to memory

Precision and formality is necessary for comprehension, but it can be too laborious for the purpose of glancing at a pair of algorithms and determining which one is more efficient. So it is worth remembering a few general principles for doing big-OOO comparison.

  1. Multiplicative constants can be dropped.
    Ex: 9n29n^29n2 is the same as n2n^2n2, opt for the simpler second term.
  2. Bigger exponents dominate, so na=O(nb)n^a = O(n^b)na=O(nb) if a<ba < ba<b.
    Ex: n2=O(n3)n^2=O(n^3)n2=O(n3).
  3. Any exponential dominates any polynomial.
    Ex: n2=O(2n)n^2=O(2^n)n2=O(2n).
  4. For exponentials, bigger bases dominate, so an=O(bn)a^n = O(b^n)an=O(bn) if a<ba < ba<b.
    Ex: 2n=O(3n)2^n=O(3^n)2n=O(3n).
  5. Any polynomial dominates any logarithm.
    Ex: log⁡n=O(n)\log n = O(n)logn=O(n).
  6. For logarithms, the base doesn't matter. From the logarithm change-of-base formula, any logarithm differs from any logarithm of a different base by just a constant factor.
    Ex: log⁡10n\log_{10} nlog10​n is the same as log⁡n\log nlogn.
